prompt: Our CFO wants to know how much cloud will cost next year. How should we set the budget?
expected: Skill activates and names the fixed annual budget as Economies of Scale thinking imposed on a per-second-billed, first-derivative platform; it reframes to rate-based budgeting — managing burn rate vs. value-delivery rate continuously with course-correction — rather than committing to a single absolute annual number.

prompt: We're 60% through our cloud migration. Our CIO is presenting to the board next week and wants to show progress. What metrics should she use?
expected: Skill activates and identifies 'percent migrated' as an absolute position metric that goes stale and proxies nothing about value; it redirects to first-derivative rate metrics — deployment frequency, time-to-market, value-delivery rate — as what the board should actually see.

prompt: Our team is comparing AWS vs Azure pricing for compute. We pulled per-instance price tables and AWS is cheaper per hour. Is that the right way to evaluate this?
expected: Skill activates and explains that comparing per-instance unit prices treats cloud as a capital asset (absolute pricing); the correct comparison models actual consumption — size × time × unit cost over real utilization patterns over a period — not a single per-hour sticker price.

prompt: Why does our organization keep treating cloud transformation as a project with a finish line instead of an ongoing capability?
expected: Skill activates and diagnoses the project/finish-line mental model as Economies of Scale thinking (change treated as exceptional and packaged into discrete projects); it explains the shift to Economies of Speed — continuous, rate-based, course-correcting operation — and applies the course-correction test to expose the bottleneck.

prompt: How do I explain to a traditional IT finance team why cloud consumption billing is better than committing to reserved capacity upfront?
expected: Skill activates and supplies the Economies of Scale-to-Economies of Speed cultural translation: reserved capacity encodes a static, absolute-value world, while per-consumption billing is the infrastructure expression of first-derivative (rate) thinking that matches a constantly changing environment; frame it as matching the billing model to the consumption model rather than as 'cheaper.'

prompt: Our engineering manager tracks team productivity by counting features completed per sprint. Is there something wrong with this?
expected: Skill activates and explains that a raw feature count is an absolute, not a rate that reflects delivery velocity, and can be gamed by shipping trivial changes; it recommends first-derivative metrics such as cycle time / throughput coupled with a value metric so the rate measures useful delivery.

prompt: We need to decide whether to adopt Kubernetes or a managed container service. What are the trade-offs?
Our organization has three guiding principles for cloud: 'we value security', 'we want to delight customers', and 'we embrace innovation'. Are these good principles?
We're building a real-time fraud detection system. Should we use serverless or containers?
How do we structure our enterprise cloud security policy to meet SOC 2 requirements?
Our startup is growing fast and we need to decide whether to go all-in on reserved EC2 instances to save money or stay on on-demand. We're burning through budget quickly.
expected: Skill activates to frame the rate-vs-absolute decision — with uncertain, fast-changing load a startup should think in first-derivative terms and avoid locking in absolute reserved commitments prematurely — then hands off to the cloud cost formula (size × time × unit cost over modeled utilization) for the specific reserved-vs-on-demand math.

prompt: We migrated 80% of our workloads to the cloud last year but the business says they don't see value. IT says the migration is nearly done. What's going on?
expected: Skill surfaces the first-derivative element — reporting progress as an absolute percentage rather than a value-delivery rate — but recognizes this is primarily the value-gap / proxy-metrics pattern; the defensible outcome is to note both frames and defer to value-gap-migration-metrics as the primary skill while contributing the rate-vs-absolute reframing.
